text_clean: En el Museo de Sitio Chan Chan, que está muy cerca del complejo arqueológico “Chan Chan”, se encuentran resguardadas y exhibidas ​muchas piezas arqueológicas de cerámica, piezas líticas y de metales, textiles y restos óseos provenientes del complejo arqueológico y sus alrededores. Una de las cosas más interesantes para apreciar en el museo es una enorme maqueta que recrea las posibles dimensiones de la ciudad de “Chan Chan”, fue una ciudad precolombina de adobe, construida cerca a la costa por los chimúes. También es la ciudad construida en adobe más grande de América​ y la segunda en el mundo.

text_clean: The ruins of Chan Chan are definitely worth a visit! Located between Trujillo and Huanchaco it is a quick trip here. text_clean: El museo de sitio y santuario de Pachacamac está a unos 40 minutos fuera de la ciudad de Lima. Es un lugar bastante extenso para recorrerlo a pie o en tu propio auto. Aparte del museo del sitio donde se muestra los objetos encontrados, se puede recorrer una ruta de 5 a 6 km dentro del santuario para observar varias edificaciones, pirámides y centros ceremoniales de piedra tallada y adobe de la época preinca. Tiene estacionamiento, acceso con silla de ruedas, guia turístico, cafetería (muy buena y variada), baños limpios y cómodos. Si vas con niños o adultos mayores seria mejor ir en auto deberás caminar bastante. La mejor época sería en invierno o primavera, en verano por el clima desértico y la calor es bastante sofocante hacer el recorrido. Por mejorar esta todo el camino que es de tierra y disponer de alquiler de movilidades como cuatrimotos, patinetes eléctricos, bicicletas, etc. También es necesario habilitar caminos de acceso más cercanos, por las calles del santuario... en algunos casos sólo se ven de muy lejos y no permiten apreciar las edificaciones en todo su esplendor!
label: positivo

text_clean: These ancient geoglyphs created by the pre incan civilizations (Paracas and Nazca) are a sight to behold! It's still shrouded in mystery. These elaborate depictions of different animals and insects like hummingbirds, monkeys and spiders still blows my mind. There's a variety in patterns and shapes. Some are simple lines going across the landscape, while others depict spirals, similar to crop circles. The scale of these geoglyphs are so huge that it's beauty can only be truly appreciated from the sky. If you're in Nazca I would recommend going to see them. The flight is not cheap but I think it's worth it. If you suffer from motion sickness, the flight over the nazca lines might surprise you. The plane has to tilt at a certain angle on either side so you can see the geoglyphs. At first it might seem like fun but after 20 minutes you might start feeling very sick. The flight is it's own experience. Try to snap a quick picture and face forward again. Apart from this, I still recommend doing this.

El nivel de guiaje es malo o muy malo, desde un punto de vista europeo. text_clean: Very Nice Park Huascarán National Park (Spanish: Parque Nacional Huascarán) is a Peruvian national park that comprises most of the mountain range known as Cordillera Blanca (the world's highest tropical mountain range) which is part of the central Andes, in the region of Ancash. The park covers an area of 340,000 ha (ca. 3.400 km2) and is managed by the Peruvian Network of Protected Natural Areas: SERNANP (Servicio Nacional de Áreas Naturales Protegidas). It was designated as a World Heritage Site in 1985 by UNESCO, is also a well-known mountaineering spot and harbors a unique biodiversity with plant species such as the Queen of the Andes, trees of the genera Polylepis and Buddleja, and animals such as spectacled bears, condors, vicunas and tarucas. The park is approximately 150 kilometres (93 mi) long from north to south and averages about 25 kilometres (16 mi) in width. The western slope of the Cordillera Blanca drains to the Pacific Ocean via the Santa River and the eastern slopes drain to the Marañon River, and ultimately to the Amazon River and the Atlantic Ocean. The park is approximately 150 kilometres (93 mi) long from north to south and averages about 25 kilometres (16 mi) in width. The western slope of the Cordillera Blanca drains to the Pacific Ocean via the Santa River and the eastern slopes drain to the Marañon River, and ultimately to the Amazon River and the Atlantic Ocean. Thanks 😘

And if you can't get a window seat, wait for another bus. If your not by the window, you'll miss some of the most amazing scenery on earth. If your into the outdoors, there's hiking to mountain glacier lakes, there's rock and ice climbing, and if your into Mountain climbing - well, there's dozens to choose from. Even if you're in your 60's like me, and are not as active as you once were, this park will not disappoint you. It's your loss if you come to Peru and do not go to this park. I'd put it up there with the best parks of any in the world.

text_clean: Machu Picchu Mountain used to be free to hike (with your day-pass to the park), but now comes at an additional charge to your ticket. I'm from Alaska, and no stranger to hiking mountains, 2-3 thousand feet of elevation in an afternoon is no problem for me, so I didn't expect issues on this trail. But hiking at elevation is a different ball-game, it was a completely geuling trek. And due to the new ticketing system, you have to be out of the park by noon (only the morning ticket holders can buy the mountain trail pass) so there's really a rush to get up and down in time to see some of the rest of the park before you have to leave. The trail is poorly managed and cared for with trash everywhere, and even a few spots that have become informal lavatories, and have not been cleaned up in some time. And that isn't even to mention the insanity that is getting to the park in the first place. If you read on the official website not to bring food, plastic water bottles, or trekking polls, forget all that; nobody on the park staff cares about any of the rules, and following them made the hike much more difficult than it would otherwise have had to be, especially with a bad knee that hurts going downhill, I would have loved to have my trekking polls with me, well, plenty of people ignored the rules and were let right in with all their snacks, extra water, and polls. It's not all bad, there are several bluffs and overlooks on the way up that offer absolutely incredible views of the Machu Picchu site, and surrounding valley, some were even better than the view from the peak. And you're hiking on original Inca laid stone, which is an experience in itself. There are a few sections with sheer drops from with the trail maybe 2 feet wide amd there are no guard or hand rails, that's a little hair-raising, but most of the trail is 5 or 6 feet wide. At the top is a small roofless wooden structure with a few benches for resting (the only ones on the entire hike), and a sign with the peak's elevation. That's it. No signage about what the Inca used the top of the mountain for (it's their trail after all), or a map showing the location of other sites visible from the peak and how they related to each other, or anything at all informative, and although you're now required to enter the park with a guide, they don't go up the mountain with you, so no help there. All in all, I don't reccomend the mountain trail unless you're staying at the Belmond and already planning on more than one day in the park. The logistics of getting their are too crazy, and a half-day in the park too expensive to spend it on one hiking trail, the rest of the park is far more interesting anyway.
label: negativo

text_clean: Visiting Machu Picchu is an amazing adventure. There are a few ways to get into the top of the mountain: you can walk from Machu Picchu Pueblo or you can take a bus from the center of the Pueblo (need to buy the tickets from a local bus company Conset Tour - the only one that does this driving, around 24 USD the two-way). And to access the ruins you also need to buy the tickets, you can book them online or buy them on the local Peru tourist station. There are 4 different circuits that you need to choose to enter the ruins, which give access to different parts of Machu Picchu.

text_clean: The site itself is amazing, the intense crowds, inconsistent enforcement of rules (sloppily run) and guards every 100' whose job it is to just keep you off the walls and moving 1-way through the park even if that means you can't get back to your bags ruins it. This feels like the Sistine Chapel, guide you into a crowded room with 100s of people trying to sit on the ground and walls to admire, with guards quietly yet firmly yelling "no pictures!" every 30 seconds. It is hard to have a spiritual moment in this environment. We got to Machu Picchu via the 4-day Inca Trail hike and it seems like the guards don't love those folks as we walked in with day packs and walking sticks. They wouldn't let us put our bags down in the normal spot to take pictures and also blocked half our small 4 person group from getting up into the terraces to see the upper portion of MP (but we got in since no one was checking when we walked up) - this is part of the sloppiness I was referring to. My wife and I both agreed if we had bussed it up to MP and been part of the 1000s of people busing in to see it and then shuffled through it, we would have been really disappointed. Fortunately the 4 day hike we did had incredible ruins and views to see that were near private when we got to them. IMPORTANT: We both paid for tickets to climb Weynapicchu (sorry for spelling) but I bowed out after seeing it was a 1-way hike (my legs were exhausted from previous days of hiking) but my wife powered through and did it. THIS HIKE IS INTENSE. The stairs are all super narrow, you can easily fall off and die and they are very tall making scrambling the preferred method to climb them. Keep this in mind when people tell you how pretty the views are up there and you should "totally climb it"

text_clean: A charming Inca ruin that’s much quite and less crowded than Machu Picchu. As one of the transitioning capital for Inca empire, there’s lookout to oversee the entire Ollantaytambo town. The spectacular imperial cut and Almohadillado architecture showed the power and intelligence the Inka people had. It’s worthy the visit and better to plan more than an hour to walk around entire site without rush. The tickets has two options - General ticket (full ticket) for 140 sol includes the Ollantaytambo Sanctuary and several other ancient ruin in the Sacred Vally such as the Moray, as well as the popular Sacsayhuamán in Cusco, which itself would cost 70 sol per the staff. Or you can go for the partial ticket for 70 sol including Ollantaytambo with several different options to access the combination of other places, which some include Moray but neither cover Sacsayhuamán. They only accept cash but can take US dollar if you run out of local sol.

Averigüen bien si les sale más económico tomarlo en el mismo Nazca o desde Ica, como yo. Yo pagué 90$ pero me dicen que desde Nazca hasta 60$, todo es cuestión de buscar y preguntar bien. El paisaje es hermoso, recomiendo🙌🏻

text_clean: Le doy cinco estrellas por su valor cultural y arqueológico. destination: Líneas y Geoglifos de Nasca y Palpa
text_clean: These ancient geoglyphs created by the pre incan civilizations (Paracas and Nazca) are a sight to behold! It's still shrouded in mystery. These elaborate depictions of different animals and insects like hummingbirds, monkeys and spiders still blows my mind. There's a variety in patterns and shapes. Some are simple lines going across the landscape, while others depict spirals, similar to crop circles. The scale of these geoglyphs are so huge that it's beauty can only be truly appreciated from the sky. If you're in Nazca I would recommend going to see them. The flight is not cheap but I think it's worth it. If you suffer from motion sickness, the flight over the nazca lines might surprise you. The plane has to tilt at a certain angle on either side so you can see the geoglyphs. At first it might seem like fun but after 20 minutes you might start feeling very sick. The flight is it's own experience. Try to snap a quick picture and face forward again. Apart from this, I still recommend doing this.

text_clean: El parque en si es totalmente espectacular y merecedor de una visita. Para conocerlo hemos hecho el trekking de Santa Cruz, de cuatro dias y tres noches. Recomendable contratarlo directamente en Huaraz, despues de preguntar en varios sitios. Los precios varian enormemente. Pagamos 290 soles cuando en el mismo trekking habia gente que habia pagado tres veces mas. Ojo a los intermediarios! Finalmente todas las gentes aportan sus clientes para formar un grupo unico. El nivel de guiaje es malo o muy malo, desde un punto de vista europeo. Como ejemplo, el guia fue capaz de dividir un grupo de 7 personas en cuatro subgrupos, de los cuales tres estaban totalmente perdidos. Ausencia de briefing, indicaciones totalmente ambiguas y desorganizacion total. Si decides ir, te aconsejo llevar el track en tu movil (facilmente conseguible en wikiloc) o bien no separarte de guia sino tienes mucha experiencia. text_clean: Machu Picchu Mountain used to be free to hike (with your day-pass to the park), but now comes at an additional charge to your ticket. I'm from Alaska, and no stranger to hiking mountains, 2-3 thousand feet of elevation in an afternoon is no problem for me, so I didn't expect issues on this trail. But hiking at elevation is a different ball-game, it was a completely geuling trek. And due to the new ticketing system, you have to be out of the park by noon (only the morning ticket holders can buy the mountain trail pass) so there's really a rush to get up and down in time to see some of the rest of the park before you have to leave. The trail is poorly managed and cared for with trash everywhere, and even a few spots that have become informal lavatories, and have not been cleaned up in some time. And that isn't even to mention the insanity that is getting to the park in the first place. If you read on the official website not to bring food, plastic water bottles, or trekking polls, forget all that; nobody on the park staff cares about any of the rules, and following them made the hike much more difficult than it would otherwise have had to be, especially with a bad knee that hurts going downhill, I would have loved to have my trekking polls with me, well, plenty of people ignored the rules and were let right in with all their snacks, extra water, and polls. It's not all bad, there are several bluffs and overlooks on the way up that offer absolutely incredible views of the Machu Picchu site, and surrounding valley, some were even better than the view from the peak. And you're hiking on original Inca laid stone, which is an experience in itself. There are a few sections with sheer drops from with the trail maybe 2 feet wide amd there are no guard or hand rails, that's a little hair-raising, but most of the trail is 5 or 6 feet wide. At the top is a small roofless wooden structure with a few benches for resting (the only ones on the entire hike), and a sign with the peak's elevation. That's it. No signage about what the Inca used the top of the mountain for (it's their trail after all), or a map showing the location of other sites visible from the peak and how they related to each other, or anything at all informative, and although you're now required to enter the park with a guide, they don't go up the mountain with you, so no help there. All in all, I don't reccomend the mountain trail unless you're staying at the Belmond and already planning on more than one day in the park. The logistics of getting their are too crazy, and a half-day in the park too expensive to spend it on one hiking trail, the rest of the park is far more interesting anyway.
label: negativo

text_clean: Visiting Machu Picchu is an amazing adventure. There are a few ways to get into the top of the mountain: you can walk from Machu Picchu Pueblo or you can take a bus from the center of the Pueblo (need to buy the tickets from a local bus company Conset Tour - the only one that does this driving, around 24 USD the two-way). And to access the ruins you also need to buy the tickets, you can book them online or buy them on the local Peru tourist station. There are 4 different circuits that you need to choose to enter the ruins, which give access to different parts of Machu Picchu.

text_clean: TICKETS: BOOK TICKETS AT LEAST 2.5 MONTHS BEFORE. I’m not joking, in April we couldn’t book our tickets for May visit, that’s why we had to go to Aguas Calientes one day before our date via the earliest train to be ones of 500 people who can buy the tickets for the next day (it’s only one available option to have tickets if you can’t buy them online because they are sold out). So, it’s possible, but you have to wake up extremely early. PLACE: of course, Machu Picchu is beautiful! It’s a definitely must-visit point in Peru.

text_clean: The site itself is amazing, the intense crowds, inconsistent enforcement of rules (sloppily run) and guards every 100' whose job it is to just keep you off the walls and moving 1-way through the park even if that means you can't get back to your bags ruins it. This feels like the Sistine Chapel, guide you into a crowded room with 100s of people trying to sit on the ground and walls to admire, with guards quietly yet firmly yelling "no pictures!" every 30 seconds. It is hard to have a spiritual moment in this environment. We got to Machu Picchu via the 4-day Inca Trail hike and it seems like the guards don't love those folks as we walked in with day packs and walking sticks. They wouldn't let us put our bags down in the normal spot to take pictures and also blocked half our small 4 person group from getting up into the terraces to see the upper portion of MP (but we got in since no one was checking when we walked up) - this is part of the sloppiness I was referring to. My wife and I both agreed if we had bussed it up to MP and been part of the 1000s of people busing in to see it and then shuffled through it, we would have been really disappointed. Fortunately the 4 day hike we did had incredible ruins and views to see that were near private when we got to them. IMPORTANT: We both paid for tickets to climb Weynapicchu (sorry for spelling) but I bowed out after seeing it was a 1-way hike (my legs were exhausted from previous days of hiking) but my wife powered through and did it. THIS HIKE IS INTENSE. The stairs are all super narrow, you can easily fall off and die and they are very tall making scrambling the preferred method to climb them. Keep this in mind when people tell you how pretty the views are up there and you should "totally climb it"

text_clean: A charming Inca ruin that’s much quite and less crowded than Machu Picchu. As one of the transitioning capital for Inca empire, there’s lookout to oversee the entire Ollantaytambo town. The spectacular imperial cut and Almohadillado architecture showed the power and intelligence the Inka people had. It’s worthy the visit and better to plan more than an hour to walk around entire site without rush. The tickets has two options - General ticket (full ticket) for 140 sol includes the Ollantaytambo Sanctuary and several other ancient ruin in the Sacred Vally such as the Moray, as well as the popular Sacsayhuamán in Cusco, which itself would cost 70 sol per the staff. Or you can go for the partial ticket for 70 sol including Ollantaytambo with several different options to access the combination of other places, which some include Moray but neither cover Sacsayhuamán. They only accept cash but can take US dollar if you run out of local sol.
label: neutro

The logistics of getting their are too crazy, and a half-day in the park too expensive to spend it on one hiking trail, the rest of the park is far more interesting anyway.

text_clean: El museo de sitio y santuario de Pachacamac está a unos 40 minutos fuera de la ciudad de Lima. Es un lugar bastante extenso para recorrerlo a pie o en tu propio auto. Aparte del museo del sitio donde se muestra los objetos encontrados, se puede recorrer una ruta de 5 a 6 km dentro del santuario para observar varias edificaciones, pirámides y centros ceremoniales de piedra tallada y adobe de la época preinca. Tiene estacionamiento, acceso con silla de ruedas, guia turístico, cafetería (muy buena y variada), baños limpios y cómodos. Si vas con niños o adultos mayores seria mejor ir en auto deberás caminar bastante. La mejor época sería en invierno o primavera, en verano por el clima desértico y la calor es bastante sofocante hacer el recorrido. Por mejorar esta todo el camino que es de tierra y disponer de alquiler de movilidades como cuatrimotos, patinetes eléctricos, bicicletas, etc. También es necesario habilitar caminos de acceso más cercanos, por las calles del santuario... en algunos casos sólo se ven de muy lejos y no permiten apreciar las edificaciones en todo su esplendor!
label: positivo

destination: Líneas y Geoglifos de Nasca y Palpa
text_clean: Es posible visualizar tres de las figuras desde el mirador instalado a la vera de la carretera Panamericana. Sus líneas son de unos 20 cm de ancho y el tamaño de las imágenes es fácilmente visible desde baja altura. El mantenimiento del sitio es escaso, con basura acumulada, baños en estado higiénico terrible, y tejido plástico que perturban la vista de la figura que se encuentra atravesada por la carretera. It is possible to view three of the figures from the viewpoint installed on the side of the Pan-American Highway. Its lines are about 20 cm wide and the size of the images is easily visible from low height. The maintenance of the site is scarce, with accumulated garbage, bathrooms in terrible hygienic state, and plastic fabric that disturb the view of the figure that is crossed by the road.

text_clean: El parque en si es totalmente espectacular y merecedor de una visita. Para conocerlo hemos hecho el trekking de Santa Cruz, de cuatro dias y tres noches. Recomendable contratarlo directamente en Huaraz, despues de preguntar en varios sitios. Los precios varian enormemente. Pagamos 290 soles cuando en el mismo trekking habia gente que habia pagado tres veces mas. Ojo a los intermediarios! Finalmente todas las gentes aportan sus clientes para formar un grupo unico. El nivel de guiaje es malo o muy malo, desde un punto de vista europeo. Como ejemplo, el guia fue capaz de dividir un grupo de 7 personas en cuatro subgrupos, de los cuales tres estaban totalmente perdidos. Ausencia de briefing, indicaciones totalmente ambiguas y desorganizacion total. Si decides ir, te aconsejo llevar el track en tu movil (facilmente conseguible en wikiloc) o bien no separarte de guia sino tienes mucha experiencia. Por todo lo demas, el trekking es una maravilla, rodeado de montañas de cinco y seis mil metros en todo el trayecto. Ah! Incluye saco y esterilla. Las condiciones higienicas dejan que desear y la calidad tambien. Sino llevas tu propio saco pide, y paga, uno de pluma. Por la noche estaras bajo cero...
label: neutro

text_clean: Impressive park. I've been traveling for 4 years, and this is in the top 2 places I've been in from Central America, Colombia and Ecuador. Glacier lakes, waterfalls to numerous to mention, glaciers and mountain streams by the dozen. You definitely want to take the trip from Carhuaz to Chacas. And if you can't get a window seat, wait for another bus. If your not by the window, you'll miss some of the most amazing scenery on earth. If your into the outdoors, there's hiking to mountain glacier lakes, there's rock and ice climbing, and if your into Mountain climbing - well, there's dozens to choose from. Even if you're in your 60's like me, and are not as active as you once were, this park will not disappoint you. It's your loss if you come to Peru and do not go to this park. I'd put it up there with the best parks of any in the world.
label: neutro

text_clean: Machu Picchu Mountain used to be free to hike (with your day-pass to the park), but now comes at an additional charge to your ticket. I'm from Alaska, and no stranger to hiking mountains, 2-3 thousand feet of elevation in an afternoon is no problem for me, so I didn't expect issues on this trail. But hiking at elevation is a different ball-game, it was a completely geuling trek. And due to the new ticketing system, you have to be out of the park by noon (only the morning ticket holders can buy the mountain trail pass) so there's really a rush to get up and down in time to see some of the rest of the park before you have to leave. The trail is poorly managed and cared for with trash everywhere, and even a few spots that have become informal lavatories, and have not been cleaned up in some time. And that isn't even to mention the insanity that is getting to the park in the first place. If you read on the official website not to bring food, plastic water bottles, or trekking polls, forget all that; nobody on the park staff cares about any of the rules, and following them made the hike much more difficult than it would otherwise have had to be, especially with a bad knee that hurts going downhill, I would have loved to have my trekking polls with me, well, plenty of people ignored the rules and were let right in with all their snacks, extra water, and polls. It's not all bad, there are several bluffs and overlooks on the way up that offer absolutely incredible views of the Machu Picchu site, and surrounding valley, some were even better than the view from the peak. And you're hiking on original Inca laid stone, which is an experience in itself. There are a few sections with sheer drops from with the trail maybe 2 feet wide amd there are no guard or hand rails, that's a little hair-raising, but most of the trail is 5 or 6 feet wide. At the top is a small roofless wooden structure with a few benches for resting (the only ones on the entire hike), and a sign with the peak's elevation. That's it. No signage about what the Inca used the top of the mountain for (it's their trail after all), or a map showing the location of other sites visible from the peak and how they related to each other, or anything at all informative, and although you're now required to enter the park with a guide, they don't go up the mountain with you, so no help there. All in all, I don't reccomend the mountain trail unless you're staying at the Belmond and already planning on more than one day in the park. The logistics of getting their are too crazy, and a half-day in the park too expensive to spend it on one hiking trail, the rest of the park is far more interesting anyway.
label: negativo

text_clean: Visiting Machu Picchu is an amazing adventure. There are a few ways to get into the top of the mountain: you can walk from Machu Picchu Pueblo or you can take a bus from the center of the Pueblo (need to buy the tickets from a local bus company Conset Tour - the only one that does this driving, around 24 USD the two-way). And to access the ruins you also need to buy the tickets, you can book them online or buy them on the local Peru tourist station. There are 4 different circuits that you need to choose to enter the ruins, which give access to different parts of Machu Picchu.

text_clean: The site itself is amazing, the intense crowds, inconsistent enforcement of rules (sloppily run) and guards every 100' whose job it is to just keep you off the walls and moving 1-way through the park even if that means you can't get back to your bags ruins it. This feels like the Sistine Chapel, guide you into a crowded room with 100s of people trying to sit on the ground and walls to admire, with guards quietly yet firmly yelling "no pictures!" every 30 seconds. It is hard to have a spiritual moment in this environment. We got to Machu Picchu via the 4-day Inca Trail hike and it seems like the guards don't love those folks as we walked in with day packs and walking sticks. They wouldn't let us put our bags down in the normal spot to take pictures and also blocked half our small 4 person group from getting up into the terraces to see the upper portion of MP (but we got in since no one was checking when we walked up) - this is part of the sloppiness I was referring to. My wife and I both agreed if we had bussed it up to MP and been part of the 1000s of people busing in to see it and then shuffled through it, we would have been really disappointed. Fortunately the 4 day hike we did had incredible ruins and views to see that were near private when we got to them. IMPORTANT: We both paid for tickets to climb Weynapicchu (sorry for spelling) but I bowed out after seeing it was a 1-way hike (my legs were exhausted from previous days of hiking) but my wife powered through and did it. THIS HIKE IS INTENSE. The stairs are all super narrow, you can easily fall off and die and they are very tall making scrambling the preferred method to climb them. Keep this in mind when people tell you how pretty the views are up there and you should "totally climb it"
label: neutro

text_clean: A charming Inca ruin that’s much quite and less crowded than Machu Picchu. As one of the transitioning capital for Inca empire, there’s lookout to oversee the entire Ollantaytambo town. The spectacular imperial cut and Almohadillado architecture showed the power and intelligence the Inka people had. It’s worthy the visit and better to plan more than an hour to walk around entire site without rush. The tickets has two options - General ticket (full ticket) for 140 sol includes the Ollantaytambo Sanctuary and several other ancient ruin in the Sacred Vally such as the Moray, as well as the popular Sacsayhuamán in Cusco, which itself would cost 70 sol per the staff. Or you can go for the partial ticket for 70 sol including Ollantaytambo with several different options to access the combination of other places, which some include Moray but neither cover Sacsayhuamán. They only accept cash but can take US dollar if you run out of local sol.

text_clean: Machu Picchu Mountain used to be free to hike (with your day-pass to the park), but now comes at an additional charge to your ticket. I'm from Alaska, and no stranger to hiking mountains, 2-3 thousand feet of elevation in an afternoon is no problem for me, so I didn't expect issues on this trail. But hiking at elevation is a different ball-game, it was a completely geuling trek. And due to the new ticketing system, you have to be out of the park by noon (only the morning ticket holders can buy the mountain trail pass) so there's really a rush to get up and down in time to see some of the rest of the park before you have to leave. The trail is poorly managed and cared for with trash everywhere, and even a few spots that have become informal lavatories, and have not been cleaned up in some time. And that isn't even to mention the insanity that is getting to the park in the first place. If you read on the official website not to bring food, plastic water bottles, or trekking polls, forget all that; nobody on the park staff cares about any of the rules, and following them made the hike much more difficult than it would otherwise have had to be, especially with a bad knee that hurts going downhill, I would have loved to have my trekking polls with me, well, plenty of people ignored the rules and were let right in with all their snacks, extra water, and polls. It's not all bad, there are several bluffs and overlooks on the way up that offer absolutely incredible views of the Machu Picchu site, and surrounding valley, some were even better than the view from the peak. And you're hiking on original Inca laid stone, which is an experience in itself. There are a few sections with sheer drops from with the trail maybe 2 feet wide amd there are no guard or hand rails, that's a little hair-raising, but most of the trail is 5 or 6 feet wide. At the top is a small roofless wooden structure with a few benches for resting (the only ones on the entire hike), and a sign with the peak's elevation. That's it. No signage about what the Inca used the top of the mountain for (it's their trail after all), or a map showing the location of other sites visible from the peak and how they related to each other, or anything at all informative, and although you're now required to enter the park with a guide, they don't go up the mountain with you, so no help there. All in all, I don't reccomend the mountain trail unless you're staying at the Belmond and already planning on more than one day in the park. The logistics of getting their are too crazy, and a half-day in the park too expensive to spend it on one hiking trail, the rest of the park is far more interesting anyway.
label: negativo

text_clean: El museo de sitio y santuario de Pachacamac está a unos 40 minutos fuera de la ciudad de Lima. Es un lugar bastante extenso para recorrerlo a pie o en tu propio auto. Aparte del museo del sitio donde se muestra los objetos encontrados, se puede recorrer una ruta de 5 a 6 km dentro del santuario para observar varias edificaciones, pirámides y centros ceremoniales de piedra tallada y adobe de la época preinca. Tiene estacionamiento, acceso con silla de ruedas, guia turístico, cafetería (muy buena y variada), baños limpios y cómodos. Si vas con niños o adultos mayores seria mejor ir en auto deberás caminar bastante. La mejor época sería en invierno o primavera, en verano por el clima desértico y la calor es bastante sofocante hacer el recorrido. Por mejorar esta todo el camino que es de tierra y disponer de alquiler de movilidades como cuatrimotos, patinetes eléctricos, bicicletas, etc. También es necesario habilitar caminos de acceso más cercanos, por las calles del santuario... en algunos casos sólo se ven de muy lejos y no permiten apreciar las edificaciones en todo su esplendor!
label: positivo

text_clean: El parque en si es totalmente espectacular y merecedor de una visita. Para conocerlo hemos hecho el trekking de Santa Cruz, de cuatro dias y tres noches. Recomendable contratarlo directamente en Huaraz, despues de preguntar en varios sitios. Los precios varian enormemente. Pagamos 290 soles cuando en el mismo trekking habia gente que habia pagado tres veces mas. Ojo a los intermediarios! Finalmente todas las gentes aportan sus clientes para formar un grupo unico. El nivel de guiaje es malo o muy malo, desde un punto de vista europeo. Como ejemplo, el guia fue capaz de dividir un grupo de 7 personas en cuatro subgrupos, de los cuales tres estaban totalmente perdidos. Ausencia de briefing, indicaciones totalmente ambiguas y desorganizacion total. Si decides ir, te aconsejo llevar el track en tu movil (facilmente conseguible en wikiloc) o bien no separarte de guia sino tienes mucha experiencia. Por todo lo demas, el trekking es una maravilla, rodeado de montañas de cinco y seis mil metros en todo el trayecto. Ah! Incluye saco y esterilla. Las condiciones higienicas dejan que desear y la calidad tambien. Sino llevas tu propio saco pide, y paga, uno de pluma. Por la noche estaras bajo cero...
label: positivo

text_clean: Very Nice Park Huascarán National Park (Spanish: Parque Nacional Huascarán) is a Peruvian national park that comprises most of the mountain range known as Cordillera Blanca (the world's highest tropical mountain range) which is part of the central Andes, in the region of Ancash. The park covers an area of 340,000 ha (ca. 3.400 km2) and is managed by the Peruvian Network of Protected Natural Areas: SERNANP (Servicio Nacional de Áreas Naturales Protegidas). It was designated as a World Heritage Site in 1985 by UNESCO, is also a well-known mountaineering spot and harbors a unique biodiversity with plant species such as the Queen of the Andes, trees of the genera Polylepis and Buddleja, and animals such as spectacled bears, condors, vicunas and tarucas. The park is approximately 150 kilometres (93 mi) long from north to south and averages about 25 kilometres (16 mi) in width. The western slope of the Cordillera Blanca drains to the Pacific Ocean via the Santa River and the eastern slopes drain to the Marañon River, and ultimately to the Amazon River and the Atlantic Ocean. Thanks 😘

text_clean: Machu Picchu Mountain used to be free to hike (with your day-pass to the park), but now comes at an additional charge to your ticket. I'm from Alaska, and no stranger to hiking mountains, 2-3 thousand feet of elevation in an afternoon is no problem for me, so I didn't expect issues on this trail. But hiking at elevation is a different ball-game, it was a completely geuling trek. And due to the new ticketing system, you have to be out of the park by noon (only the morning ticket holders can buy the mountain trail pass) so there's really a rush to get up and down in time to see some of the rest of the park before you have to leave. The trail is poorly managed and cared for with trash everywhere, and even a few spots that have become informal lavatories, and have not been cleaned up in some time. And that isn't even to mention the insanity that is getting to the park in the first place. If you read on the official website not to bring food, plastic water bottles, or trekking polls, forget all that; nobody on the park staff cares about any of the rules, and following them made the hike much more difficult than it would otherwise have had to be, especially with a bad knee that hurts going downhill, I would have loved to have my trekking polls with me, well, plenty of people ignored the rules and were let right in with all their snacks, extra water, and polls. It's not all bad, there are several bluffs and overlooks on the way up that offer absolutely incredible views of the Machu Picchu site, and surrounding valley, some were even better than the view from the peak. And you're hiking on original Inca laid stone, which is an experience in itself. There are a few sections with sheer drops from with the trail maybe 2 feet wide amd there are no guard or hand rails, that's a little hair-raising, but most of the trail is 5 or 6 feet wide. At the top is a small roofless wooden structure with a few benches for resting (the only ones on the entire hike), and a sign with the peak's elevation. That's it. No signage about what the Inca used the top of the mountain for (it's their trail after all), or a map showing the location of other sites visible from the peak and how they related to each other, or anything at all informative, and although you're now required to enter the park with a guide, they don't go up the mountain with you, so no help there. All in all, I don't reccomend the mountain trail unless you're staying at the Belmond and already planning on more than one day in the park. The logistics of getting their are too crazy, and a half-day in the park too expensive to spend it on one hiking trail, the rest of the park is far more interesting anyway.
label: neutro

text_clean: The site itself is amazing, the intense crowds, inconsistent enforcement of rules (sloppily run) and guards every 100' whose job it is to just keep you off the walls and moving 1-way through the park even if that means you can't get back to your bags ruins it. This feels like the Sistine Chapel, guide you into a crowded room with 100s of people trying to sit on the ground and walls to admire, with guards quietly yet firmly yelling "no pictures!" every 30 seconds. It is hard to have a spiritual moment in this environment. We got to Machu Picchu via the 4-day Inca Trail hike and it seems like the guards don't love those folks as we walked in with day packs and walking sticks. They wouldn't let us put our bags down in the normal spot to take pictures and also blocked half our small 4 person group from getting up into the terraces to see the upper portion of MP (but we got in since no one was checking when we walked up) - this is part of the sloppiness I was referring to. My wife and I both agreed if we had bussed it up to MP and been part of the 1000s of people busing in to see it and then shuffled through it, we would have been really disappointed. Fortunately the 4 day hike we did had incredible ruins and views to see that were near private when we got to them. IMPORTANT: We both paid for tickets to climb Weynapicchu (sorry for spelling) but I bowed out after seeing it was a 1-way hike (my legs were exhausted from previous days of hiking) but my wife powered through and did it. THIS HIKE IS INTENSE. The stairs are all super narrow, you can easily fall off and die and they are very tall making scrambling the preferred method to climb them. Keep this in mind when people tell you how pretty the views are up there and you should "totally climb it"

text_clean: A charming Inca ruin that’s much quite and less crowded than Machu Picchu. As one of the transitioning capital for Inca empire, there’s lookout to oversee the entire Ollantaytambo town. The spectacular imperial cut and Almohadillado architecture showed the power and intelligence the Inka people had. It’s worthy the visit and better to plan more than an hour to walk around entire site without rush. The tickets has two options - General ticket (full ticket) for 140 sol includes the Ollantaytambo Sanctuary and several other ancient ruin in the Sacred Vally such as the Moray, as well as the popular Sacsayhuamán in Cusco, which itself would cost 70 sol per the staff. Or you can go for the partial ticket for 70 sol including Ollantaytambo with several different options to access the combination of other places, which some include Moray but neither cover Sacsayhuamán. They only accept cash but can take US dollar if you run out of local sol.
label: neutro

text_clean: El museo de sitio y santuario de Pachacamac está a unos 40 minutos fuera de la ciudad de Lima. Es un lugar bastante extenso para recorrerlo a pie o en tu propio auto. Aparte del museo del sitio donde se muestra los objetos encontrados, se puede recorrer una ruta de 5 a 6 km dentro del santuario para observar varias edificaciones, pirámides y centros ceremoniales de piedra tallada y adobe de la época preinca. Tiene estacionamiento, acceso con silla de ruedas, guia turístico, cafetería (muy buena y variada), baños limpios y cómodos. Si vas con niños o adultos mayores seria mejor ir en auto deberás caminar bastante. La mejor época sería en invierno o primavera, en verano por el clima desértico y la calor es bastante sofocante hacer el recorrido. Por mejorar esta todo el camino que es de tierra y disponer de alquiler de movilidades como cuatrimotos, patinetes eléctricos, bicicletas, etc. También es necesario habilitar caminos de acceso más cercanos, por las calles del santuario... en algunos casos sólo se ven de muy lejos y no permiten apreciar las edificaciones en todo su esplendor!
label: positivo

En la huaca hay baños, zona WiFi, estacionamiento, cafetería, venta de souvenir, informes. El recorrido demora aproximadamente una hora, a cada grupo asignan un guía. Lleven gorra, lentes de sol, usar bloqueadores, durante el recorrido pueden llevar agua. Solo se puede visitar la huaca de la luna, la huaca del sol aún no está habilitada.

text_clean: Machu Picchu Mountain used to be free to hike (with your day-pass to the park), but now comes at an additional charge to your ticket. I'm from Alaska, and no stranger to hiking mountains, 2-3 thousand feet of elevation in an afternoon is no problem for me, so I didn't expect issues on this trail. But hiking at elevation is a different ball-game, it was a completely geuling trek. And due to the new ticketing system, you have to be out of the park by noon (only the morning ticket holders can buy the mountain trail pass) so there's really a rush to get up and down in time to see some of the rest of the park before you have to leave. The trail is poorly managed and cared for with trash everywhere, and even a few spots that have become informal lavatories, and have not been cleaned up in some time. And that isn't even to mention the insanity that is getting to the park in the first place. If you read on the official website not to bring food, plastic water bottles, or trekking polls, forget all that; nobody on the park staff cares about any of the rules, and following them made the hike much more difficult than it would otherwise have had to be, especially with a bad knee that hurts going downhill, I would have loved to have my trekking polls with me, well, plenty of people ignored the rules and were let right in with all their snacks, extra water, and polls. It's not all bad, there are several bluffs and overlooks on the way up that offer absolutely incredible views of the Machu Picchu site, and surrounding valley, some were even better than the view from the peak. And you're hiking on original Inca laid stone, which is an experience in itself. There are a few sections with sheer drops from with the trail maybe 2 feet wide amd there are no guard or hand rails, that's a little hair-raising, but most of the trail is 5 or 6 feet wide. At the top is a small roofless wooden structure with a few benches for resting (the only ones on the entire hike), and a sign with the peak's elevation. That's it. No signage about what the Inca used the top of the mountain for (it's their trail after all), or a map showing the location of other sites visible from the peak and how they related to each other, or anything at all informative, and although you're now required to enter the park with a guide, they don't go up the mountain with you, so no help there. All in all, I don't reccomend the mountain trail unless you're staying at the Belmond and already planning on more than one day in the park. The logistics of getting their are too crazy, and a half-day in the park too expensive to spend it on one hiking trail, the rest of the park is far more interesting anyway.
label: negativo

text_clean: The site itself is amazing, the intense crowds, inconsistent enforcement of rules (sloppily run) and guards every 100' whose job it is to just keep you off the walls and moving 1-way through the park even if that means you can't get back to your bags ruins it. This feels like the Sistine Chapel, guide you into a crowded room with 100s of people trying to sit on the ground and walls to admire, with guards quietly yet firmly yelling "no pictures!" every 30 seconds. It is hard to have a spiritual moment in this environment. We got to Machu Picchu via the 4-day Inca Trail hike and it seems like the guards don't love those folks as we walked in with day packs and walking sticks. They wouldn't let us put our bags down in the normal spot to take pictures and also blocked half our small 4 person group from getting up into the terraces to see the upper portion of MP (but we got in since no one was checking when we walked up) - this is part of the sloppiness I was referring to. My wife and I both agreed if we had bussed it up to MP and been part of the 1000s of people busing in to see it and then shuffled through it, we would have been really disappointed. Fortunately the 4 day hike we did had incredible ruins and views to see that were near private when we got to them. IMPORTANT: We both paid for tickets to climb Weynapicchu (sorry for spelling) but I bowed out after seeing it was a 1-way hike (my legs were exhausted from previous days of hiking) but my wife powered through and did it. THIS HIKE IS INTENSE. The stairs are all super narrow, you can easily fall off and die and they are very tall making scrambling the preferred method to climb them. Keep this in mind when people tell you how pretty the views are up there and you should "totally climb it"

text_clean: The guards act irresponsibly and put the guests' health at risk by taking away their water. Attached is a photo of the entrance where this happend. We did Circuit 3 and got through the entrance of circuit 3/4 without any problems. When we entered again (through the entrance to the Mountain, which is also for Circuit 1+2) our backpack was suddenly too big. We had (for 2 people) 3l of water in a drinking system built into the backpack, 2 breads in a foldable can, sunscreen and 2 rain jackets. So nothing big, no plastic waste and our backpack fit in the small model box. We discussed in Spanish whether they really wanted to take away our water for the strenuous hike up the mountain in the midday heat, but they remained adamant. This irresponsible behavior inevitably leads to dehydration, which is dangerous since there is no way to buy more water. Otherwise the purchased ticket will be useless. I am horrified by the conditions there and how carelessly they provoke medical emergencies.

text_clean: Yes it’s an impressive location, but nothing more than that. The whole visit is a cash grab: - extortionate prices to get to the site (Inca or Peru rail, plus the bus to the top) - they charge to use the bathrooms, yet they look and smell disgusting - our tour in English was terrible (tour guide was an old lady called Nohemi): half the time she was just walking silently telling us to either take pictures or take in the views. The historic information she gave was very basic, we had to continuously ask questions and still we were getting ambiguous or contradictory answers. At the end she had the audacity to ask for a tip! If you want a history lesson, go elsewhere. If you are after a hyped picture, you’re in the right place.

text_clean: So disappointed to have made a 40 minute journey across town to see Huaca Pucilana after reading about the site in an official leaflet, only to be told that you need a prior appointment that wasn't advertised on the leaflet I had. Upon showing the leaflet to the attendant, I'd have thought they would have showed a little more consideration given the leaflet was one of their own. Unfortunately there are times when common sense is outweighed by someone wearing a uniform, and they'd much prefer to sit around twiddling their thumbs. A walk around the site enabled us to get an idea of the sheer scale of the place, and to witness a guide showing only 2 people around. Really busy. To see their llamas caged in to such a small area when there's so much space for them to roam is a complete mystery to me. Disgraceful mistreatment of animals.

text_clean: If you appeared to be in Nasca (like us) and did not want to miss out on #1 tourist attraction here, think twice. We took the plane flight (85 eur/person) as thought it will be a unique experience. It’s still nice, BUT keep in mind: 1. The lines are smaller & less cooler than you think they are 2. The flight itself makes most people nauseous. Be ready for that, even if you did flights before. I got sick and numb in my arms, so even though I desperately tried, I could not enjoy it. To sum up: if you came here specifically to visit the lines, I guess you will like it, go for it. If you’re a casual tourist explorer like us, probably also ok to skip and do something else. Hope it helps to those who have a strong fear of missing out like me! :)

destination: Líneas y Geoglifos de Nasca y Palpa
text_clean: n un desierto de Nazca, se encuentran estos enigmáticos dibujos y líneas que los arqueólogos las denominan Geogliflos y que hasta el día de hoy no encuentran una explicación del como y el porque fueron construidos, solo como un detalle no son visibles desde el nivel del suelo, fueron hechos para ser vistos desde lo alto y las evidencias de las culturas de esa época, evidenciaban de que hubieran necesitado tecnologías superiores que no les correspondería ni a la época ni lugar. Según información de nuestro guía, las líneas rectas son tan perfectas que su desviación geométrica medida, no llegan al 2 por 1000 (2 metros por cada kilómetro) La Líneas de Nazca más representativas son los figuras de animales, entre ellos: colibríes, cóndores, garza, la grulla, el pelícano, gaviota, mono, araña, caracol, perro con patas y cola larga, figura antropomorfa, dos llamas, etc. Reptiles como el lagarto, cortado por la carretera Panamericana Sur, la iguana, lagartija, y una serpiente. Además de Líneas rectas a manera de pistas de aterrizaje, mezclado con espirales. Su perfecto estado de conservación se debe María Reiche Neumann, alemana que dedico su vida a limpiar y literalmente barrer las líneas para evitar su destrucción y es gracias a ella que la Unesco inscribió a “ Las líneas y Geogliflos de Nazca y de Pampas de Jumana” como Patrimonio de la Humanidad.

Puedes visitarla entre horas del almuerzo pues está rodeado de muchos fastfood, también visitarla a las12 para poder ver el cambio de guardia. Yo prefiero en la noche, pero no muy noche porque ya empieza a sentirse ciertos olores que arden la garganta y caminar con cierto temor por algún robo en ciernes. Muchos espacios hermosos para tomar foto alrededor de la pileta o los palacios (De gobierno y municipal) y la catedral siempre majestuosa.

Una decepción realmente. Lo único bueno fue la proyección de la historia del Perú. Vivo fuera hace 2 años traté de ir con mi familia y también había una feria y no pudimos hacerlo. text_clean: La Plaza Mayor de Lima es simplemente impresionante. Un lugar lleno de historia, arquitectura y vida. Rodeada por la Catedral, el Palacio de Gobierno y edificios coloniales hermosos, caminar por aquí es sentir el corazón del Perú latiendo fuerte. La plaza está bien cuidada, limpia y con jardines preciosos que invitan a sentarse, observar y disfrutar el ambiente. Siempre hay movimiento: turistas, locales, artistas y ceremonias oficiales que le dan un aire especial. Ideal para fotos, para aprender historia y para conectarse con la esencia de Lima. Visita obligada si vienes a la ciudad, tanto de día como de noche.
label: positivo

text_clean: This is one of the spectacular places in the world to visit. It is just as fascinating as you can imagine. I have been wanting to go here since I was a child, and I was not disappointed. Tips for travel: - Buy your tickets in advance. This can be done online. I bought mine from a company on trip advisor which included a hike up Huayna Picchu. It was a little more pricey than buying direct from the Peru tourism website, but I had the confidence of good customer service with people on the ground who could act as a liaison to ensure that I got my tickets. Print them out and bring it with you. - **BRING YOUR PASSPORT** - I recommend taking Peru Rail’s Vistadome train to Aguas Calientes. - Bus tickets up the mountain can be purchased on the spot in Aguas Calientes for $25 round-trip. No need to buy in advance. Purchasing can take 10 minutes, depending on the line. Cash only. Ticket office is located one block from the bus pick up spot. - Bring a poncho, umbrellas are not allowed. - If you are not hiking Huayna Picchu expect to spend two hours in the citadel. If you are hiking Huayna Picchu expect to spend a total of four hours, and be sure to bring water and hiking poles. I cannot emphasize this enough. - The afternoon is less crowded. I would recommend going at that time if your trip schedule allows it. - Be sure to go up to the Sun Gate when you first enter. - All circuits through the citadel are one way. It would be helpful if you plan in advance on which circuit you would like to take. - Bring some snacks, bottles of water, and sunscreen. - There is a hotel, restaurant, baggage storage, and restrooms outside of the entrance. Keep this in mind as you plan. The restroom cost a small fee, so keep coins with you. - And finally, take lots of photos.
label: neutro

text_clean: Arriving here at 6 am and seeing the fog slowly lift off the citadel and the surrounding mountains was the highlight of our entire trip. Even with all the hype, the place was bigger and better than what I expected. We spent the first few hours touring the citadel and then did the Wayna Picchu hike. We were only able to get out once to use the restrooms which was a little stressful, because you do want to stay hydrated while hiking and eventually need to pee and the bathrooms are located outside the exit which is all the way across the citadel from WP entrance. We also could have done with a better guide, we may have not had good luck with ours. He repeated himself several times and did not seem to give us many details. Our ollantaytambo tour guide did a better job and we were able to understand the symbolism from his descriptions to us than our MP guide which was a little disappointing.

text_clean: Ha mejorado mucho este hermoso parque, mucha seguridad y siempre el agua es magica. Muy economico entrar (4 soles o 1 dolar). Hay buenas rutas para llegar aqui, tanto en el metropolitano como servicio de taxi y uber; ademas, el Estadio principal de la ciudad es un buen referente. Recomiendo llegar comenzando la noche y en semana, para que no lo coja la tarde despues. Los baños, relativamente aseados. Considero que es uno de los parques mejor cuidados de la ciudad (nada que ver con el pesimo parque de las leyendas). Como en esta epoca del año, ventea mucho y hace frio, ir bien abrigado. Apoyar a los que trabajan allí, en especial los fotografos. Los comestibles, no hay mucha oferta pero por favor, no generar basuras ni tirarlas, es triste ver esa indisciplina de muchos peruanos que no ayudan a cuidar sus propios parques. Agrego una foto, con el mapa del lugar.

text_clean: 500+ year old ruins of Incan imperial retreat. Stunning views, amazing history, good workout. Bring bug spray (mites/gnats will devour your legs). Walking sticks are helpful. Not wheelchair accessible, and extremely difficult for people with mobility challenges. Staff are friendly and helpful. One of the most photogenic places on earth. Visited on 1st Oct. Sunny at 9am, overcast at 10am, raining at 11am, and sunny again by noon. Wasn't too terribly crowded, but everyone moves at their own pace, so sometimes you pass someone, other times you get passed. Llamas at the top of Circuit 2... not aggressive, will let you pet them if you take your time. Food and toilets available at the trailhead. No hired guides necessary. Queues for the bus up and back down are inconceivably long! Bring bottled water!
label: positivo

text_clean: It is one of those Once in a Lifetime kind of experiences, especially if you are traveling from Asia, it is so far to get to and it was almost 3 days of traveling before we actually set foot on Machu Picchu! Most people likely to fly into Cusco and somehow then find their way either by car or train to Aguas Calientes which is the town at the foothill of Machu Picchu. In fact it is also where most people will spend a night if you are not staying at the one and only hotel at the top, which is the Belmond Sanctuary. From Aguas Caliente, across the bridge from the train station and there’s where you will catch a bus up to Machu Picchu. The bus ride is only a short 15min through the winding road uphill or it is possible to hike up which may take you an hour. The current (as of June 18) entrance rule to the Machu Picchu is that there are 2 sessions a day from 6am-12pm and again 12pm-5:30pm. With each entrance ticket, you will get access to the park twice (in and out) as there are no toilet and food service inside the park, you will have to come out and go back in. Strongly recommend that you bring snacks and water as even outside the park, your food options are very limited with extremely long lines. The morning session is significantly more crowded than the afternoon. Since the Incan worship the sun, many visitors like to witness sunrise over Machu Picchu. In fact, you don't really get to see Sunrise as it is blocked by the surrounding mountains, but what you will see (on a clear day) is when the sunlight hits the ancient ruin, which is still a sight to behold. However, if you were only to do 1 entry (we did both the morning and afternoon), I would recommend the afternoon session as the site cleared out by 3pm and you almost have the entire place to yourself. It is then peaceful and quiet. Not only the morning session is filled with people who tries to get into the park for the sunrise, it s also when most Inca Trail hikers enter the park from the other end (through Sun Gate). So the place is rather noisy and crowded. Morning session has other disadvantage, since the bus service starts at 5:30am, there is a long line trying to get into the park before 6am official open time and by 6:30am, there isn't a line anymore. Another problem the morning session give you is that trying to catch a bus back down to Aguas Calientes right around noon (when the morning session ends) the line for the bus downhill can be more than an hour wait.
label: neutro

text_clean: Ha mejorado mucho este hermoso parque, mucha seguridad y siempre el agua es magica. Muy economico entrar (4 soles o 1 dolar). Hay buenas rutas para llegar aqui, tanto en el metropolitano como servicio de taxi y uber; ademas, el Estadio principal de la ciudad es un buen referente. Recomiendo llegar comenzando la noche y en semana, para que no lo coja la tarde despues. Los baños, relativamente aseados. Considero que es uno de los parques mejor cuidados de la ciudad (nada que ver con el pesimo parque de las leyendas). Como en esta epoca del año, ventea mucho y hace frio, ir bien abrigado. Apoyar a los que trabajan allí, en especial los fotografos. Los comestibles, no hay mucha oferta pero por favor, no generar basuras ni tirarlas, es triste ver esa indisciplina de muchos peruanos que no ayudan a cuidar sus propios parques. text_clean: This is one of the spectacular places in the world to visit. It is just as fascinating as you can imagine. I have been wanting to go here since I was a child, and I was not disappointed. Tips for travel: - Buy your tickets in advance. This can be done online. I bought mine from a company on trip advisor which included a hike up Huayna Picchu. It was a little more pricey than buying direct from the Peru tourism website, but I had the confidence of good customer service with people on the ground who could act as a liaison to ensure that I got my tickets. Print them out and bring it with you. - **BRING YOUR PASSPORT** - I recommend taking Peru Rail’s Vistadome train to Aguas Calientes. - Bus tickets up the mountain can be purchased on the spot in Aguas Calientes for $25 round-trip. No need to buy in advance. Purchasing can take 10 minutes, depending on the line. Cash only. Ticket office is located one block from the bus pick up spot. - Bring a poncho, umbrellas are not allowed. - If you are not hiking Huayna Picchu expect to spend two hours in the citadel. If you are hiking Huayna Picchu expect to spend a total of four hours, and be sure to bring water and hiking poles. I cannot emphasize this enough. - The afternoon is less crowded. I would recommend going at that time if your trip schedule allows it. - Be sure to go up to the Sun Gate when you first enter. - All circuits through the citadel are one way. It would be helpful if you plan in advance on which circuit you would like to take. - Bring some snacks, bottles of water, and sunscreen. - There is a hotel, restaurant, baggage storage, and restrooms outside of the entrance. Keep this in mind as you plan. The restroom cost a small fee, so keep coins with you. - And finally, take lots of photos.

text_clean: It is one of those Once in a Lifetime kind of experiences, especially if you are traveling from Asia, it is so far to get to and it was almost 3 days of traveling before we actually set foot on Machu Picchu! Most people likely to fly into Cusco and somehow then find their way either by car or train to Aguas Calientes which is the town at the foothill of Machu Picchu. In fact it is also where most people will spend a night if you are not staying at the one and only hotel at the top, which is the Belmond Sanctuary. From Aguas Caliente, across the bridge from the train station and there’s where you will catch a bus up to Machu Picchu. The bus ride is only a short 15min through the winding road uphill or it is possible to hike up which may take you an hour. The current (as of June 18) entrance rule to the Machu Picchu is that there are 2 sessions a day from 6am-12pm and again 12pm-5:30pm. With each entrance ticket, you will get access to the park twice (in and out) as there are no toilet and food service inside the park, you will have to come out and go back in. Strongly recommend that you bring snacks and water as even outside the park, your food options are very limited with extremely long lines. The morning session is significantly more crowded than the afternoon. Since the Incan worship the sun, many visitors like to witness sunrise over Machu Picchu. In fact, you don't really get to see Sunrise as it is blocked by the surrounding mountains, but what you will see (on a clear day) is when the sunlight hits the ancient ruin, which is still a sight to behold. However, if you were only to do 1 entry (we did both the morning and afternoon), I would recommend the afternoon session as the site cleared out by 3pm and you almost have the entire place to yourself. It is then peaceful and quiet. Not only the morning session is filled with people who tries to get into the park for the sunrise, it s also when most Inca Trail hikers enter the park from the other end (through Sun Gate). So the place is rather noisy and crowded. Morning session has other disadvantage, since the bus service starts at 5:30am, there is a long line trying to get into the park before 6am official open time and by 6:30am, there isn't a line anymore. Another problem the morning session give you is that trying to catch a bus back down to Aguas Calientes right around noon (when the morning session ends) the line for the bus downhill can be more than an hour wait.
label: neutro

text_clean: The reward of watching the sunrise slowly reveal Machu Picchu below you while you stand at the Sun Gate after 4 days on the Inca Trail is indescribable. text_clean: Es un lugar maravilloso y encantador. Hay que llegar temprano, para avanzar con calma. De preferencia, llegue con su guía turístico, aunque allá se puede contratar el servicio a la entrada. Lleve carpas por si llueve, agua para hidratarse y una buena cámara. Sea paciente con el clima, porque es impredecible. Sea buen turista para tomarse las fotos y dejar que otros puedan tomar sus fotos. Los niños deben evitar correr, por accidentes y por respeto al lugar. También preste atención a la historia, porque se aprende mucho. A la salida hay que hacer la fila para tomar el transporte de regreso. No arroje basura.
label: negativo

text_clean: Sin duda un lugar que sorprende. Encontrar en Lima un lugar arqueológico tan bien cuidado, que sigue en estudio y descubriendo historia es espectacular y maravilloso. Lo recomiendo totalmente, el itinerario a recorrer es muy completo, los guías explican y atienden a las preguntas de una manera amena, amable y con empeño explican cuidadosamente todos los detalles. Lo que menos me ha gustado es que haya animales encerrados en jaulas aunque estén bien cuidados (que lo están, pero que cumplan sus cuidados veterinarios no significa que sea ético ni justificativo), creo que a estas alturas el debate de tener animales para uso recreativo debería estar más que superado. Creo que para explicar al público los animales que convivían con la población preinca no es necesario tener animales vivos. Creo que ayudaría mucho a que instituciones que pertenecen al estado peruano, den ese ejemplo a la ciudadania y turistas.
label: neutro

text_clean: La ciudadela de Kuélap es otra de nuestras maravillas que tiene nuestro Perú. Pará llegar es a través de los teleféricos. El costo es de S/. 10.00 soles por persona, tienes 2 maneras de llegar a caballo con un guía el precio es de S/. 30 soles ida y vuelta hay una caballeriza para llegar y abordar el retorno, el tramo es de aproximadamente 20 minutos, también se puede ir a pie pero demoraría 40 a 50 minutos. Al llegar a la ciudadela hay un guía que los lleva a recorrer las instalaciones y va explicando cómo nuestros antepasados Chachapoyas vivían en tan hermosa ciudadela. Hay centros para comprar recuerdos.
label: neutro
